title: The connection between autoimmune conditions and childhood adversity
author: Kalesh

The body remembers what the mind tries to forget

htmlContent:
The body remembers what the mind tries to forget

I was twenty-seven when my hands started swelling. Not the kind of swelling you notice and forget about. The kind where you can't make a fist in the morning. Where your wedding ring leaves a deep red groove in your skin by noon. The kind that makes you wonder if you're falling apart for no reason at all.

For two years I went to doctors. Rheumatologists. Immunologists. A specialist who charged four hundred dollars to tell me my inflammation markers were "elevated but not diagnostic." Another who suggested I try cutting out gluten. Another who asked if I was "under a lot of stress" in the same tone you'd ask someone if they'd been drinking too much coffee.

Here's the thing. Nobody asked me what happened to me when I was a child. Nobody asked about my nervous system. Nobody connected the dots between the trauma I'd spent years pretending didn't exist and the way my immune system had started attacking my own tissue.

But the dots were there. They're always there.

I'm not a doctor. I'm not a researcher. But I've read enough studies and sat in enough support groups to know that the link between childhood adversity and autoimmune conditions isn't a fringe theory anymore. It's one of the most well-documented, least-discussed realities in modern medicine.

Let me be direct with you. If you're living with an autoimmune condition and you've experienced significant childhood trauma, your body is not betraying you. It's trying to protect you. It just got its signals crossed somewhere along the way.

The science that connects your history to your symptoms

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk has spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. His work at the Trauma Research Foundation has shown that people who experienced childhood abuse or neglect have significantly higher rates of autoimmune disorders than the general population. We're not talking about a small difference here. We're talking about a 50 to 100 percent increase in risk depending on the severity and duration of the trauma.

Think about that. Your childhood experiences may have literally reprogrammed your immune system.

How does this work? Stay with me here. When you're a child and you experience chronic stress, your body releases cortisol. That's the stress hormone. In small doses, cortisol helps you survive. It mobilizes energy. It sharpens your focus. It tells your body to fight or flee.

But here's the problem. Children can't fight or flee from caregivers. They can't run away from their own parents. So the stress stays. The cortisol stays elevated. And eventually, your body's stress response system starts breaking down.

Dr. Gabor Mate has written extensively about this. He calls it the "biology of loss." His research suggests that when children suppress their authentic emotions to survive in dysfunctional families, that suppression creates a physiological cost. Your immune system starts making mistakes. It can't tell the difference between a real threat and a perceived one. It starts attacking healthy tissue because it's been trained to see danger everywhere.

I remember reading Mate's work and feeling something crack open in my chest. For years I'd been told my autoimmune condition was "idiopathic." That's a fancy medical term for "we have no idea why this is happening." But I knew. I'd always known. The shame I carried from my childhood wasn't just in my head. It was in my joints. In my gut. In the way my skin reacted to things that shouldn't have bothered it.

What inflammation actually means in a traumatized body

Let's talk about inflammation. You've heard that word a thousand times. It's in every wellness article, every supplement ad, every Instagram post about "healing your gut." But I want you to think about inflammation differently for a moment.

Inflammation is your body's way of saying something is wrong. It's a signal. A cry for help. When you twist your ankle, inflammation rushes to the site to start the healing process. That's normal. That's healthy.

But when your body is chronically inflamed without an obvious injury, something deeper is going on. Your nervous system is stuck in a state of high alert. Your immune system is producing inflammatory markers as if there's a constant threat. And the thing is, there is a threat. It's just not a threat in the present moment. It's a threat that lives in your memory. In your cells. In the way your body learned to survive.

Dr. Stephen Porges developed something called the Polyvagal Theory. It explains how your nervous system constantly scans for safety and danger. When you grew up in an environment that wasn't safe, your nervous system learned to default to survival mode. Your vagus nerve, which helps regulate your immune response, stopped working the way it was supposed to.

This is why trauma survivors often have such intense physical symptoms. Your body isn't making this up. It's not trying to punish you. It's trying to protect you using the only strategies it knows.

I've found that simple tools can help reset this system. A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is the Breathing Exercise Tool. It sounds too simple to work, but extending your exhale literally tells your vagus nerve that you're safe. I use mine when I feel that familiar tightness in my chest, the one that used to send me into a spiral of panic about my health.

Right? It feels almost absurd that something so simple could make a difference. But the research backs it up. Lengthening your exhale activates the parasympathetic nervous system. It's like pressing a reset button on your stress response.

Why the wellness industry gets this so wrong

I need to be honest with you about something that makes me angry. The wellness industry has turned autoimmune conditions into a moral failing. You hear things like "you just need to heal your trauma" or "your thoughts are creating your disease" or "if you could just lower your stress, your symptoms would disappear."

That's not just unhelpful. It's cruel.

Your autoimmune condition is not your fault. You didn't it through negative thinking. You didn't attract it into your life. Your body learned to respond to an unsafe world, and now it's stuck in that response. That's not a character flaw. That's a survival adaptation that outlived its usefulness.

I've seen so many people in support groups who are drowning in shame. They believe that if they could just "heal enough," their symptoms would vanish. And when they don't vanish, they blame themselves. They think they're not trying hard enough. Not meditating enough. Not doing enough yoga.

Here's what I want you to hear. Healing from trauma can reduce your symptoms. It can lower your inflammation. It can help your nervous system find more safety. But it may not cure your autoimmune condition. And that's okay. That doesn't mean you're failing.

What matters is that you stop fighting your body and start understanding it. That you learn to listen to what it's telling you without judgment. That you find ways to signal safety to your nervous system, even when your brain doesn't believe you're safe.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score. Van der Kolk lays out the science in a way that's accessible without being simplistic. Reading it felt like someone finally validated what I'd been experiencing for years. I wasn't crazy. My body was just doing what it had to do to survive.

And for those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine. Levine developed Somatic Experiencing, which is a way of releasing trapped trauma from the body without having to talk about every detail of what happened. It's been incredibly helpful for people whose autoimmune flares seem tied to emotional triggers they can't control.

What actually helps when your body is on fire

I'm not going to give you a ten-step plan. I'm not going to tell you that if you just do these three things, you'll be healed. That's not how this works. But I can tell you what has helped me and what I've seen help others.

First, stop trying to fix your body. I know that sounds counterintuitive. But the more you fight your symptoms, the more you signal danger to your nervous system. Your body interprets your fear as proof that something is wrong. And it responds by producing more inflammation. It's a vicious cycle.

Instead, try curiosity. When you feel a flare coming on, notice what's happening without judgment. Where is the sensation in your body? What shape is it? Does it have a color? A temperature? This isn't about making the sensation go away. It's about changing your relationship to it.

Second, find ways to regulate your nervous system that don't require you to be "healed" first. Many trauma survivors struggle with meditation because sitting still with your thoughts can be terrifying. That's okay. You don't have to meditate. You can walk slowly. You can rock in a chair. You can listen to music that makes you feel safe.

For some people, reducing sensory input makes a huge difference. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs. I wear mine when I'm in crowded spaces or when the sound of my own breathing feels too loud. It's a small thing, but it helps my nervous system settle.

Third, find a therapist who understands both trauma and the body. Not all therapists are created equal. Look for someone trained in Somatic Experiencing, EMDR, or Internal Family Systems. These modalities understand that trauma lives in the body, not just in the mind. They won't tell you to just "think positive." They'll help you gently release what your body has been holding.

Fourth, give yourself permission to rest. Real rest. Not the kind where you're scrolling your phone while lying in bed. The kind where you let your body fully relax. This is hard for trauma survivors because relaxation can feel dangerous. Your nervous system has learned that letting your guard down means getting hurt. But slowly, over time, you can teach it that safety is possible.

An ending that isn't neat

I wish I could tell you that understanding the connection between childhood adversity and autoimmune conditions will make everything better. That once you know why your body is doing what it's doing, the symptoms will disappear. But that's not how it works.

What does happen is something quieter. Something harder to measure. You stop hating your body for betraying you. You start seeing its symptoms as messages instead of attacks. You learn to hold your own experience with compassion instead of contempt.

And sometimes, that compassion is enough to calm the fire. Not always. Not completely. But enough that you can breathe. Enough that you can sleep. Enough that you can look at yourself in the mirror and see someone who is surviving, not someone who is broken.

Your body is not your enemy. It's been trying to keep you alive since the day you were born. It just needs new information. It needs to learn that the danger is over. That you're safe now. That it can finally, finally rest.

It takes time. It takes patience. It takes a kind of gentleness that our culture doesn't value and that trauma survivors often don't know how to give themselves. But it's possible. I've seen it happen. I'm watching it happen in my own body, one slow breath at a time.
